topic: Someone's childhood imaginary friend appears in their niece's drawings
raw_output:
[story 1]

Eleanor stared at the stack of crayon drawings spread across the kitchen table. Her niece, Lily, had been unusually imaginative lately, filling the pages with swirling, impossible creatures and grinning, vaguely menacing figures. Eleanor picked up one—a spindly, multi-limbed shadow with too many eyes—and let out a short, sharp exhale. It felt like a physical irritation. "Honestly," she muttered to the empty room, tapping the paper with a perfectly manicured nail, "the sheer lack of originality in this kid's output is staggering." She folded the drawing with unnecessary force, the crinkle of the paper sounding loud in the quiet afternoon. There was no warmth in the gesture, only a cool assessment of something she found utterly beneath her notice.

[story 2]

I watched Maya painstakingly shade the edges of her latest sketch. The figure in the corner—a creature with drooping ears and an expression of profound boredom—was undeniably peculiar. A tight knot formed in my stomach, a feeling of profound dismissal washing over me. *What is this nonsense?* I thought, leaning back in my chair, crossing my arms so tightly my knuckles turned white. When Maya finally held up the paper, her eyes wide with excitement, I offered a tight, almost imperceptible nod. "It's... certainly detailed," I said, my voice clipped and devoid of inflection. I made a show of examining the drawing for flaws, my gaze lingering just long enough to signal that I had seen it, but not long enough to suggest any genuine engagement.

[story 3]

The sheer absurdity of the drawing made my jaw tighten. It was a grotesque amalgamation of things I couldn't quite decipher, rendered in bright, childish hues. I picked up the paper, the texture of the cheap paper feeling flimsy beneath my fingertips. A faint sneer played on my lips as I surveyed the artwork. *Such effort expended on such trivialities.* I didn't bother to look closely at the details; I just wanted it gone. With a swift, almost dismissive flick of my wrist, I crumpled the page into a tight ball and tossed it toward the recycling bin. The sound of the paper hitting the metal was the only acknowledgment I offered that the image had existed at all.
